tier: Semantic vs. Instance vs. Panoptic Segmentation
title: Semantic Segmentation
concept: Semantic segmentation classifies every pixel in an image into a class, but does not distinguish between different instances of the same class.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does image segmentation differ from object detection?

options.text: It makes predictions at the pixel level rather than drawing boxes
It runs on classical CPUs instead of neural networks
It is only used for black and white photos

cards.title: Counting sheep or painting grass
cards.body: If you look at a field of sheep, you might want to know where the grass is, where the sky is, and where the sheep are. You don't necessarily care about naming each sheep 'Sheep A' or 'Sheep B'. You just want to label all sheep pixels with a single color. This is semantic segmentation.

cards.title: Semantic Segmentation
cards.body: Semantic Segmentation is a pixel-level classification where all pixels belonging to the same class are given the exact same label. If there are five dogs in an image, they are all colored with the same dog mask.

cards.title: Highlighting text
cards.body: Think of semantic segmentation like using highlighters. You highlight all verbs in yellow, nouns in blue, and adjectives in green. You aren't separating individual words; you're just color-coding their grammatical category.

cards.title: Satellite land cover analysis
cards.body: Satellites scan forests, water bodies, and urban zones. Semantic segmentation is used to label every pixel as 'Forest' (green), 'Water' (blue), or 'Urban' (grey) to track deforestation or city growth over time.

cards.title: No individual identity
cards.body: A common myth is that semantic segmentation can count objects. It can't! Since all overlapping objects of the same class merge into one big mask, the model cannot distinguish where one object ends and another begins.

left.text:
Paints all car pixels red.
No boundary between overlapping cars.
Great for background context.

right.text:
Needs instance segmentation to distinguish.
Separate labels for individual cars.
Better for tracking.

exercises.prompt: What happens to multiple objects of the same class in semantic segmentation?

options.text: They are all colored with the exact same class label and merge if overlapping
Each object gets a unique ID and different color
The model ignores them and only segments the background
Only the largest object of that class is segmented

exercises.explain: Correct! Semantic segmentation does not distinguish between individual instances of the same class.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Semantic segmentation is ideal if you need to count the exact number of apples in a pile.
exercises.explain: False. Since overlapping apples will merge into one single mask, counting them with semantic segmentation is extremely difficult.

exercises.sentence: In semantic segmentation, two overlapping pedestrians will be colored with ___ mask color.
exercises.explain: Because it classifies by category, not by individual object instance.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a semantic segmentation task?

options.text: Coloring all pixels representing grass in a field green
Finding each individual car and giving them separate color masks
Locating a cat with a bounding box

exercises.explain: Coloring all grass pixels green is a class-wide task, which is semantic segmentation.

exercises.prompt: You are building a system to monitor deforestation using satellite images. You need to calculate the total acreage of forest. Which approach is best?

options.text: Semantic segmentation, because you only care about the total area of the forest class, not identifying individual trees.
Instance segmentation, because you must count every single tree.
Object detection, because you need to draw boxes around all forests.
Image classification, because you only need to know if the image contains any green pixels.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Semantic segmentation is perfect for mapping background 'stuff' like forests, roads, and water where individual counts are not needed. Next, let's learn how to separate individual objects!
